General safety and regulatory
Conventions for safety and regulatory information
1.1 Conventions for safety and regulatory information
1.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 1.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 1.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.

This equipment is in compliance with applicable product safety standards for the countries to
which it is supplied.
If necessary, contact Elekta Limited for more information on the applicable safety standards.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4 Important safety instruction
It is important to install, operate, and do the maintenance correctly on all the equipment. This is
most important for safety related items. For the safety of the User and patients, Elekta
recommends that the User read, understand, and obey:
• All warnings, cautions, and notes in this document and related documents
• All warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of
the equipment
• All safety instructions and information in this document and related documents.
Elekta recommends that:
• All Users obey the precautions in this section
• All Users examine the warnings, cautions, and notes in this document and use them in
their local Work Instructions.
Before the User operates, or do work on the equipment, it is important that they read and
understand the emergency procedures.
1.4.1 Using this document
WARNING 1.3
Do not use this document to operate the equipment or to do the repair and maintenance work.
If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
Only use this document together with the applicable user and service documentation. Before you
operate the equipment or do the repair and maintenance work, read the warnings, cautions and
safety precautions in the Instructions for Use and corrective maintenance documentation.
1.4.2 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 1.4
Do not use parts or accessories unless they are supplied or approved by Elekta. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the
performance of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or
decrease the immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
1.4.3 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702160_08
Corrective Maintenance Manual - Accessories
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 11

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID
systems when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 1.5
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.6
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical
mistreatment.
1.4.4 Safety precautions for operation of the equipment
WARNING 1.7
Do not operate the equipment unless the QA checks are satisfactorily completed. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.8
Do not operate the equipment if it is damaged, defective, or incorrectly calibrated. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the
equipment.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
Only operate the equipment if you are a qualified person with the applicable authority. Such
operation must apply best practice for clinical treatment and obey the laws and regulations in the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Before you operate the equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective parts.
• The equipment calibration is correct.
• The applicable QA checks are satisfactorily completed.
• The user routine checks are satisfactorily completed.
• The planned maintenance schedule is completed satisfactorily.
For information on the user routine checks and the planned maintenance schedule, see the
applicable section of the Instructions for Use.
It is the responsibility of the User to set up a QA program that gives safe and satisfactory system
operation.
1.4.5 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 1.9
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.10
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct
• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
1.4.6 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 1.11
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, or other safety devices
unless special instructions in this document tell you to. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4.7 Safety precautions for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 1.12
Do not start to do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the power to the work area
at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.13
Do not remove the covers or cables from the equipment unless special instructions in this
document tell you to. The covers give access to high voltage electric parts. Always install the
covers and cables again before you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause
fatal injury.
WARNING 1.14
Do not remove the covers from the equipment unless special instructions in this document tell
you to. The covers give access to parts that move. Always install the covers again before you
use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.15
Be careful when the power supply switch of the equipment is put in the off position. There
continues to be a risk of high voltage electric shock. Some of the customer interfaces can
continue to supply electrical power to the electrical terminals. If you ignore this warning, it can
cause fatal injury.
Only operate the equipment in rooms that are in compliance with the applicable electrical safety
laws and regulations for this type of equipment.
Before you start the maintenance and repair work, or start to clean the equipment, isolate the
electrical power supply and prevent unwanted movement of the equipment.
1.4.8 Safety precautions for work with UV devices
WARNING 1.16
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the UV device without protective glasses. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Some devices use ultraviolet lighting. The covers and screens of the device contain the ultraviolet
lighting to the inner parts of the device.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the UV device, switch off the digital accelerator and
contact your Elekta representative.
Document ID: 45133702160_08 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual - Accessories

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4.9 Safety precautions for work with laser devices
WARNING 1.17
Do not look into the laser beams. The result can be damage to your eyes. If you ignore this
warning, it can cause serious injury.
1.4.10 Safety precaution for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 1.18
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
1.4.11 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
WARNING 1.19
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer,
do not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
1.4.12 Precautions for work with electrostatic discharge sensitive devices
CAUTION 1.2
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded antistatic wrist
strap. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.3
Do not touch the parts identified with the ESD caution label. Always obey the ESD precautions
during work on these parts. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
Semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and the parts that contain them, are ESD sensitive
devices. These devices are electrostatic discharge sensitive (ESDS) devices. ESD can cause damage
to these devices and cause them to have a malfunction immediately or some time after the
electrostatic discharge. To prevent the ESD damage to these devices, always wear a grounded
antistatic wrist strap, and obey the applicable ESD precautions and procedures.
Elekta recommends that all users receive the training about ESD. The training must include the
ESD caution label and these ESD precautions.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4.13 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
WARNING 1.20
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1.4.14 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 1.21
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray when the equipment is connected to
the electrical supply. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.22
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.4
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.5
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room with
disinfectants. Elekta recommends that the procedures to clean the equipment and the
equipment room obey the applicable laws and regulations of the jurisdiction where the
equipment is installed.
1.4.15 Safety precaution for the occurrence of fire or explosion
WARNING 1.23
Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
High levels of oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, can make an explosive
or flammable environment in the room.
1.4.16 General cleaning of the equipment
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room. This section
gives information on the general procedures. Where special procedures and precautions are
necessary, the related documents give the information on these procedures.
Clean surfaces of the equipment with a mild detergent solution. Then, dry the surfaces with a
clean, lint-free cloth.
Document ID: 45133702160_08 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual - Accessories
Page 16 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
Do not let the sprays, water, or other liquids go into the equipment. These can cause electrical
short circuits, metal corrosion, and other damage.
Do not use a solution, solvent, abrasive detergent, or polish that is corrosive. If you do not know
the properties of the solution, do not use it.
1.4.17 Recommendations for emergency procedures
Before you use the equipment, Elekta recommends that you know all the local emergency
procedures and the locations of all the emergency switches.
The Elekta equipment includes:
• The STOP MOTORS buttons on the handheld controllers and user interface panels in
the treatment room, and FKP in the control room.
When you press these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply from the motors,
stops all movements of the equipment, and stops kV and MV radiation.
• The EMERGENCY POWER OFF buttons in the treatment room, control area, and areas
near the equipment.
When you operate these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply to the linear
accelerator. The buttons disconnect the electrical supply from many electrical circuits,
but not from all the circuits.
1.4.18 Disposal of the equipment
WARNING 1.24
Do not discard radioactive and hazardous material through the domestic waste systems or in
the environment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
environment.
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Always use procedures that give the best possible protection to the environment when you
remove and discard the equipment. The procedures must obey the laws and regulations of the
jurisdictions in which you discard the equipment. Do not discard Elekta equipment through the
domestic waste systems.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, must remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material. Do not discard material that is hazardous to
health and the environment together with other material.
Before you remove and discard equipment, contact Elekta for information.

Introduction
CMM Document scope
2.1 CMM Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question.
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas.
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units).
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(customer lifecycle management) case or an ECR (Engineering change Request) and assign it to
the relevant queue.

Product description
Electron applicators
3.1.1 Description of the different electron applicators
An electron applicator is attached to the digital accelerator for electron beam treatments. The
electron applicators help deliver uniform, sharply defined treatment fields of the necessary
dimensions.
A large range of electron applicators are available, with a selection of square, rectangular or
circular cross sections. There are four types of electron applicator:
• Square
• Rectangular (includes the short SSD electron applicator for arc therapy)
• Null (blank base)
• Circular (tube).
WARNING 3.1
Make sure that you only use the accessories attached to the BLD in their safe work limits. If you
ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, or damage to the
equipment.
The conditions for safe work limits for accessories attached to the BLD are:
• A force greater than 250 N must not be applied on the hook or latch on the accessory
ring
• Accessories must weigh less than 50 kg
• The center of mass of an accessory must not be more than 80 mm from the BLD
accessory ring.
013180_001
Figure 3.7 Square and circular electron applicators
Note: When you attach an electron applicator to the BLD in Clinical Mode, it disables the ASU function.

Product description
Electron applicators
3.1.2 Description of the attachment of an end frame to the square and
rectangular electron applicators
The square and rectangular electron applicators have removeable end frames.
WARNING 3.2
Do not attach end frames with a weight of more than 7 kg. If you ignore this warning, it can
cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
The end frame slides into two guide rails at the end of the applicator. Two cam operated clamps
hold the coded end frame securely against the fixed end frame of the applicator. A safety spring
prevents the coded end frame from falling out when the clamps are released.
3.1.3 Description of the field size display for the square and rectangular
electron applicators
The BLD leaves and diaphragms are automatically set to the best position for the selected
electron applicator and energy.
For square and rectangular electron applicators, the field size display varies according to the
setting of preset potentiometers RVI and RV2 on the BLD. The correct setting of these
potentiometers is essential for accurate auto-tracking of the leaves and diaphragms.
3.1.4 Description of the end frame coding for the square and rectangular
electron applicators
Coding holes on the end frame operate the coding switches on the electron applicator to identify
the end frame to the TCS when the applicator is fitted to the BLD.
Table 3.1 Square and rectangular electron applicator end frame codes
Code description Code number
Electron applicator attached with end frame fitted incorrectly (fault 0
code)
Square and rectangular electron applicators standard end frames 1
Square and rectangular electron applicators custom end frames 2 to 14
Electron applicator attached without end frame fitted (fault code) 15
Elekta supplies end frames at the same nominal size as the electron applicator. It is also possible
to make custom irregular end frame shapes from applicable shielding block material. The
irregular end frame shapes can be the same shape as the actual treatment area. The irregular
end frames replace the standard end frame. You can use a maximum of 14 end frame codes for
each electron applicator. Contact your local Elekta representative for information on Aktina
electron applicator end frame moulds.

Electron applicators
3.1.5 Description of the touchguard of square and rectangular electron
applicators
Spring-loaded pillars, which compress when pressure is applied, hold the end frames. If the
springs compress by 3 mm to 5 mm, they activate a microswitch. The microswitch stops all motor
drives to the digital accelerator and treatment table, and terminates radiation.
3.1.6 Description of the attachment of an end tube to the circular (tube)
electronic applicator
The end tube fits into a spring-loaded retainer mechanism at the end of the electron applicator.
To fit an end tube into the circular electron applicator, the coding grooves on the end tube must
match the coding guide blocks in the selector ring. Spring loading is provided by the spring and
the sliding tube assembly. A retaining spring prevents the coded end tube from falling out.
3.1.7 Description of the end tube coding to the circular (tube) electronic
applicator
The circular electron applicator has a set of removeable stainless steel end tube inserts. The
inserts are coded and identified with 2, 3, 4, and 5 (their size in cm).
Table 3.2 Circular electron applicator end tube codes
Code description Code number
Electron applicator attached with end tube fitted incorrectly (fault 0
code)
Circular electron applicator standard end tubes 2 to 5
Electron applicator attached without end tube fitted (fault code) 15
Microswitches SW1 to SW5 are mounted on flats machined into the cylindrical body of the
electron applicator. The electron applicator body supports the selector ring, and is centralized
within the outer tube by three spacers. The spacers are attached to the body and shimmed to
give concentricity between the selector ring and the outer tube. The body is attached to the
outer tube by three pairs of fixing screws which pass through holes in the spacers (see Figure
3.8).

3.2.3 Description of the principle of operation
The electron applicator and accessory system is represented in System Diagrams Manual,
drawing 4513 330 7036 for the assymetric diaphragms BLD, drawing 4513 332 7065 for the MLCi/
MLCi2, and drawing 1023971 for Agility™. The system has two functions:
• To identify the accessory type to the control system
• To control the diaphragms or leaves.
The electron applicator or shadow tray identifies itself to the system via connector SK26D on the
BLD. The inputs are arranged into four groups:
• A seven-bit code, representing the applicator type and the end frame or tube fitted. On
the assymetric diaphragms BLD, this code is input to a high stability resistor (HSR)
ladder network, and the resulting voltage is passed to i387 via the SCC-RHA PCB and the
AI12-RHA. On the MLCi (serial number up to and including 141097) this data is passed
to the software item via the MLCi RTU I/O PCB, on MLCi serial number 141098 onwards
and all MLCi2, the data is passed via the Head Electronics (old and new part numbers
see footnote). On Agility™ this data is passed via the router PCB.
• A two-bit accessory type code. On the assymetric diaphragms BLD, this code is
passed to i385 and i386 via two analog channels on the AI12-RHA PCB. On the
MLCi (serial number up to and including 141097) this data is passed to the
software items via the MLCi RTU I/O PCB, on MLCi serial number 141098 onwards
and all MLCi2, the data is passed via the Head Electronics. On Agility™ this data is
passed via the router PCB.
• The nominal electron applicator field size (X and Y values). The size is set by
adjusting RV1 and RV2 on the electron applicator. On the assymetric diaphragms
BLD, this data is passed to i100 and i105 respectively via two analog channels on
the AI12-RHA PCB. On the MLCi (serial number up to and including 141097) this
data is passed to the software items via the MLCi RTU I/O PCB, on MLCi serial
number 141098 onwards and all MLCi2, the data is passed via the Head
Electronics. On Agility™ this data is passed via the router PCB.
• i204 detects the presence of an electron applicator from the +24 V appearing on
SK26D pin 12.
The inputs from SK26D are processed to the following software items:
• i040 Appl. type (applicator type)
• i041 Appl No. (applicator number)
• i042 Acsy. No. (accessory number).
Old part numbers - MTU 4513 390 2704 / Analog I/O 4513 390 2734 / Distribution 4513 390 4714
New part number - 4513 332 8500

3.2.4 Description of the electrical interface
All electron applicators use a common interface with the control system via PL26D. The various
circuit functions are therefore grouped under PL26D pin numbers as detailed in the following
sections.
3.2.4.1 Description of the logic supply
Pins 5 and 13 are used for the 10 V and 0 V logic supplies.
Note: Pin 3 is also 0 V except on the SL-75 series systems, where it is used to apply a -10 V reference
voltage to RV1 and RV2.
3.2.4.2 Description of the electron applicator and the shadow tray code
Pins 8, 10, 23, 19, 20, 21 and 22 carry the binary codes representing:
• The electron applicator type and end frame number (electron applicators only)
• The shadow tray number (shadow tray only).
On electron applicators the type code is hard wired. All the remaining codes are customer
selectable via the coding switches.
MLCi/MLCi2™
The coded data is passed to the MLCi RTU I/O PCB (on older MLCis) or Head Electronics (on
newer MLCi and all MLCi2™) which outputs serial data across the BLD retractile cables to i387.
Agility™
The coded data is passed to the router PCB which outputs CAN data across the BLD retractile
cables to i387.

3.2.4.4 Description of the field size of pins 4 and 9
Pins 4 and 9 are used only on electron applicators. The pins deliver the output of preset
potentiometers RV1 and RV2 defining electron applicator field size:
• Pin 4 = DRG_Xapp_siz_start (i100)
• Pin 9 = DRG_Yapp_siz_start (i105).
These items inform the control system of the size of electron applicator fitted, for example, when
automatically tracking the diaphragm position for a specific electron energy.
3.2.4.5 Description of pins 11 and 12 - electron applicator is present
Pins 11 and 12 are used to loop back a +24 V signal only when an electron applicator is present.
This signal, i204, is used as part of an interlock which makes sure that electrons cannot be
delivered in the absence of an electron applicator and X-rays cannot be delivered in the presence
of an electron applicator.
3.2.4.6 Description of pins 17 and 18 - touchguard interlock
Pins 17 and 18 link the touchguard switches on the electron applicator into the touchguard safety
interlock circuit on the BLD.

3.3 Shadow trays
3.3.1 Description of the different shadow trays
A shadow tray can be attached to the digital accelerator for X-ray beam treatments. The shadow
tray lets you put shielding blocks in the treatment field. This allows further beam shaping and
enables irregular shaped fields to be delivered safely.
There are two types of shadow tray:
• Standard shadow tray
• Short shadow tray.

WARNING 3.3
Do not put more than 30 kg of shielding blocks on to a shadow tray for gantry angles of 0° or
180°. For all other gantry angles, do not put more than 15 kg of shielding blocks on to a shadow
tray. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 3.4
Do not continue unless you make sure that the shadow tray will not be in a collision with
objects or persons. The shadow tray extends in front of the touchguard and if it comes into
contact with an object, the machine does not automatically stop. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 3.5
Do not continue unless you make sure that the accessories are used in their safe work limits. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and clinical mistreatment.
The conditions for safe work limits for accessories attached to the BLD are:
• A force greater than 250 N must not be applied on the hook or latch on the accessory
ring
• Accessories must weigh less than 50 kg
• The center of mass of an accessory must not be more than 80 mm from the BLD
accessory ring.

Product description

WARNING 3.6
Do an inspection of the acrylic trays before each use. Make sure that the trays are not
damaged, for example the trays must not contain cracks and must be sufficiently transparent.
Discard defective equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury or clinical
mistreatment.
WARNING 3.7
Make sure that the latch operates correctly, and there is no movement of the accessory, before
each use. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, or damage
to the equipment.
During X-ray treatment it is often necessary to use shielding blocks. These are shaped pieces of
lead or a similar material that are used to shield sensitive organs within the field defined by the
beam limiting leaves and diaphragms. The shielding blocks can be spring-loaded between the two
acrylic trays or attached permanently to a tray.
The coded acrylic tray slides into two guide slots at the end of the shadow tray. The tray is locked
in place by a catch with a lever release.
The removeable acrylic tray (the tray nearest to the radiation source) is removable by releasing
four knurled release screws.
3.3.6 Description of the limited operational life of the shadow tray acrylic
trays
The acrylic trays used on the shadow tray have a limited operational life. The two factors that
limit the life of a tray are:
• Distortion - which results from the loss of weight-bearing strength
• Opaqueness - which indicates a deterioration of the plastic structure.
3.3.7 Description of the acrylic tray coding
Coding holes on the coded acrylic tray operate the coding switches on the shadow tray to identify
the acrylic tray to the TCS when the shadow tray is attached to the BLD.
Table 3.14 Shadow tray codes
Code description Code number
No shadow tray attached (fault code) 0
Shadow tray attached with coded acrylic tray installed 1 to 126
Shadow tray attached without coded acrylic tray (fault code) 127
Shadow tray attached with coded acrylic tray installed incorrectly 128
(fault code)
Note: Code 127 will be shown if a shadow tray is attached without an acrylic tray, or if an acrylic tray is
installed that has all the holes drilled. Elekta recommends that code 127 is not used for any acrylic
tray.

Product description
Shadow trays
Elekta supplies acrylic trays with the shadow tray. Additional acrylic trays are available from
Elekta. A tool to make the code holes is supplied with them. It is also possible to make custom
coded acrylic trays in-house. The custom coded acrylic trays replace the standard coded acrylic
tray.
WARNING 3.8
Make sure that in-house acrylic trays are made correctly, and are a good fit to the shadow tray.
Make sure that the acrylic tray locking latch operates correctly. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
Note: Codes 0 and 128 are invalid codes. If the acrylic tray code is 0 or 128, radiation will be inhibited
and the error Perspex Tray Fault will appear on the View Restrictions dialog box. It is not possible
to use an acrylic tray with code 0. Make sure that the shadow tray and coded acrylic tray are
correctly attached.

3.3.8 Description of the identification codes for coded acrylic trays
An acrylic tray must have an identification code. The code is a maximum of seven holes, made
with a drill in a special sequence, on the side of the acrylic tray. The valid range is 0 to 126.
Acrylic trays can be made in-house.
Coded acrylic trays, and acrylic trays without identification codes, are available from Elekta.

3.5 Description of the optical distance meter (ODI)
The optical distance meter is attached to an adjustable mounting located on the gantry arm. The
device projects a scale to indicate the source-to-surface distance (SSD) over the range 75 cm to
170 cm to an accuracy ±3 mm.

Troubleshooting
Description of the inhibits of the TCS
4.1 Description of the inhibits of the TCS
The TCS compares details of the electron applicator prescribed in the patient record, with details
of the electron applicator that is actually installed. This information is obtained by decoding the
electron applicator outputs. If there is a discrepancy, all movements and the delivery of radiation
are inhibited.
The control system also generates the inhibits in response to electron applicator related faults:
i042 Acsy. No (accessory number)
Refer to Description of the i042 accessory number inhibit
i041 Appl. No. (applicator number)
Used for electron treatment only, this item indicates a fault in the wiring to pins 15 and 16 of
PL26D on the electron applicator - the hardwired accessory type code.
i2169 Accy Fitment (accessory fitment)
This item indicates that an electron applicator end frame or a shadow tray has been incorrectly
fitted, or was not installed at all.
i204 Appl pres. (applicator present)
This item indicates that an electron applicator is present and an X-ray treatment (or an X-ray
check radiograph) was selected, or no electron applicator is present and an electron treatment
was selected.
i566 X2 val var app
i567 Y2 val var app
Diaphragms X1, X2, Y1 and Y2 have not finished auto-tracking to their optimum position for field
flatness/low leakage, or an electron beam has been selected but no electron applicator is
installed.
i100 Appl.X
i105 Appl.Y
The indicated electron applicator size is in excess of its maximum permitted value. In this event it
is necessary to adjust the electron applicator preset potentiometers RV1 and RV2.
i205 Touchguard
This item indicates normal touchguard operation, or that a circular electron applicator is installed
but no end tube is present.
4.2 Description of the i042 accessory number inhibit
If code 127 is prescribed and a coded acrylic tray is attached incorrectly (fault code 128), then
treatment delivery is inhibited by item i042.
Note: Code 127 will be shown if a shadow tray is attached without a coded acrylic tray. It is
recommended that code 127 is not used for any coded acrylic tray.
1 Clear inhibit i042 as follows:
a Remove the coded acrylic tray.
b Remove the shadow tray.
c Install the shadow tray again.

Troubleshooting
Examining the accessories after a collision
4.3 Examining the accessories after a collision
Elekta shadow trays and electron applicators are designed to withstand normal operating
conditions. In the unlikely event of an accident or collision between an accessory and an
obstruction (for example, the treatment table or the floor), Elekta recommends that the
following procedure is followed:
1 Do not use the accessory until it has been thoroughly inspected by Elekta service personnel
who are certified to carry out this task.
2 Notify Elekta as soon as possible for this investigational inspection to take place.
3 Make sure that the latch components on the BLD are examined for damage and security
before other accessories are attached.
4 If significant damage has occurred, make sure that the entire shadow tray or electron
applicator is replaced.
4.4 Correcting electron applicators and shadow trays not
latched on the BLD
The electron applicators and shadow trays must be carefully latched on the BLD. If the electron
applicators and shadow trays are not carefully latched into position then there is a risk that they
can fall.
1 Do a check of the accessory hook and latch.
2 Make sure that the accessory is latched correctly on the BLD.
3 If the accessory has fallen, examine it for damage and replace it if necessary.
Related Links:
Doing a check of the accessory hook and latch on page 91
4.5 Correcting circular electron applicator end tubes not
latched
The end tube must be carefully latched into the body of the electron applicator. If the end tube is
not carefully latched into position, there is a risk that it can fall.
1 Do a check of the circular electron applicator end tube retaining springs.
2 Make sure that the end tube is latched correctly into the body of the electron applicator.
3 If the end tube has fallen, examine it for damage. If applicable, adjust or repair the retaining
springs.
Related Links:
Repairing the end tube retaining spring of the circular electron applicator on page 69
Doing a check of the end tube retaining springs of the circular electron applicator on page 91
4.6 Correcting a blurred ODI scale
The blurred SSD figures are most probably caused by too much heat around the ODI lamp.

Service parts repair or replacement
Repairing the end tube retaining spring of the circular electron applicator
5.1 Repairing the end tube retaining spring of the circular
electron applicator
1 Hold the end cap in place and remove the four M2.5 x 10 countersunk screws that hold it.
Note: Do not lift the end cap from the main body of the electron applicator as the copper springs can fall
out and be lost.
2 Invert the electron applicator while you hold the end cap in place. Remove the end cap
while the electron applicator is inverted so that the eight copper springs remain in position.
3 Make sure that each of the four latch arrangements has a minimum dimension of 2.25 mm
between the 66 mm internal diameter of the end cap and the faces of the four retaining
springs.
a Measure from the spring faces nearest to center of the tube (see Figure 5.1).
b If the selected latch arrangement does not let you apply the minimum dimension of
2.25 mm, decrease the length of the copper springs by the minimum amount necessary
to achieve the requirements shown in Figure 5.1.

6.5 Calibrating the auto-tracking system
6.5.1 Description of the calibration procedure for the auto-tracking
system
For each electron applicator, the diaphragm positions which give optimum flatness for each
energy can be learnt, and these values are individually saved. When an electron applicator is
fitted, the diaphragms automatically move to the saved values, according to the energy selected.
For Integrity™ R1.x the MLCi/MLCi2 leaves can be moved to a predetermined position, although
it is recommended to leave them at their default position. For Integrity™ R3.0 the Agility™ leaves
move to a predetermined position. It is recommended to leave them at their default positions.
Values can be saved for circular electron applicators with reference to the associated end tube
number. This value is echoed in the field size value displayed. It may be necessary to change the
read-back value for i040 and i041 to confirm the circular electron applicator and end tube
fitment number.
The procedure to adjust the electron applicator diaphragm size is slightly different depending
upon the version of Integrity™ software.
6.5.2 Calibrating the auto-tracking for Agility™
1 Attach the necessary electron applicator.
2 Open the Deliver Quick Beam panel.
3 Set the Radiation Type to Electrons.
4 Select the necessary electron energy.
5 Confirm the beam settings.
6 Select the MLC primary icon.
7 Select the Auto Tracking secondary icon.
8 In the Auto Tracking panel, type the values taken from the tables for the selected energy
and electron applicator size into the X1, X2, Y1, and Y2 fields.
Note: The values in Table 6.3, Table 6.4 and Table 6.5 are the recommended values for optimum
performance. They can be used as start values, and cover both IEC and USA flatness.
Note: Elekta recommends that the sizes for the leaves are left at the default values.
9 Select Save.
This saves the new auto-tracking settings.
Note: If you change the auto-tracking settings, you must do a check of the electron flatness for the
energy and electron applicator. See the Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual -
Beam Physics for more information.

6.6.1 Doing a check of the electron applicators and shadow trays
WARNING 6.1
Make sure that in-house acrylic trays are made correctly, and are a good fit to the shadow tray.
Make sure that the acrylic tray locking latch operates correctly. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
1 Make sure that in-house acrylic trays are made correctly, and are a good fit to the shadow
tray.
2 Make sure that the acrylic tray locking latch operates correctly.
6.6.2 Doing a check of the circular electron applicator coding
microswitches
1 Set the multimeter to a resistance range and connect to the appropriate pins of PL26D.
2 Connect a spare mating receptacle into PL26D to facilitate the connection of test leads.
3 Install each end tube in turn, rotate the selector ring to each of the four detent positions.
4 Make sure that the relevant coding switches operate consistently, even when the end float
of the selector ring is taken up within its working clearance:
– Pin 5 if the expected bit value is 1
– Pin 14 if the expected bit value is 0.
6.6.3 Doing a check of electron applicator size X and Y values
Table 6.24 Software items

6.6.4 Doing a check of the accessory hook and latch
1 Operate the latch T-handle on the accessory.

3 Release the latch T-handle.
4 Make sure that the latch T-handle goes back to the rest position and the latch tongue moves
back to its extended position.
Note: Free movement of the latch T-handle in the direction of travel can show that the tongue of the
latch is stuck.
5 If the latch mechanism does not operate correctly, do not use the accessory.
6 Put the digital accelerator in the Preparatory state and do these steps:
a Attach an electron applicator or shadow tray to the BLD.
b Operate the latch T-handle on the accessory to break the latch. Make sure that the
buzzer goes on.

6.6.5 Doing a check of the end tube retaining springs of the circular
electron applicator
1 Rotate the selector ring to each of the four detent positions.
a At each detent position, do a check for a minimum dimension of 2.25 mm between the
66 mm internal diameter of the end cap and the faces of the four retaining springs.
b Measure from the spring faces nearest to centre of the tube (see Figure 6.2).
2 If a dimension is less than 2.25 mm, do the procedure Repairing the end tube retaining
spring of the circular electron applicator.

6.8 Other accessories
6.8.1 Setting up the front pointer
The front pointer must indicate the machine isocenter to within ± 1 mm, and the values indicated
in the test conditions to within ± 2 mm.
1 Make sure that the crosswires were set correctly at the isocenter, at a maximum of ±1 mm
diameter locus with 360° BLD rotation.
2 Put a piece of graph paper on the treatment table top and align the paper with the
projected crosswire image.
3 On the graph paper, mark the position of the crosswire image in the G-T direction - a line of
approximately 10 cm.
4 Make a small mark of approximately 1 cm in the A-B direction of the crosswires.
5 Set the gantry to 0°, then attach the front pointer.
6 Apply a thin layer of Loctite® 290 to the screw threads of the clamp fixing screws.
7 Set up a fixed pointer to protrude from the tabletop in the G-T direction. Make sure that the
fixed pointer aligns with the tip of the front pointer without making contact with the front
pointer during a 360° rotation of the gantry.
8 Rotate the gantry to 180° and set the height of the treatment table so that the fixed pointer
is mid-way between the positions of the front pointer at 0° and 180°.
The fixed pointer defines the isocentric height.
9 Rotate the gantry to 0° and do a check of the indication of the front pointer.
10 If the indication is incorrect, do the steps that follow:
a Loosen the two grub screws on the side of the assembly to adjust the height of the
magnet.
b Reset the magnet so that the front pointer indicates exactly 100.
c Tighten the two grub screws on the side of the assembly.

11 Rotate the BLD through 360° and make sure that the tip of the front pointer does not
deviate from the indication of the crosswire image by more than ±1 mm.
12 If the error is more than ±1 mm, do the steps that follow:
a Rotate the BLD through 360° and mark the errors at the four cardinal angles.
b Move the pointer on the entire magnet assembly, to the left or right, by twisting the
assembly.
c Once positioned at the correct orientation, tighten the 10 mm nut inside the assembly
through the access hole until it is quite stiff.
d Loosen the two allen bolts that secure the magnet sufficiently to allow forward and
backward movement of the magnet assembly without disturbing the previously set
height adjustment.
Note: These two bolts are located at the rear of the assembly, behind the magnet.
e Once correctly positioned, tighten the two allen bolts again.
13 Move the treatment table up by 15 cm and do the steps that follow:
a Do a check of the indication of the front pointer on the graph paper.
b Rotate the BLD through 360°.
c Make sure that the tip of the front pointer does not deviate from the crosswire image
by more than ± 2 mm.

6.8.2.2 Setting the ODI to isocenter
WARNING 6.3
Do not touch the ODI lamp assembly and adjacent parts, until they are cool. The ODI lamp
assembly and the adjacent parts can be very hot when the lamp is switched off. If you ignore
this warning, it can cause serious injury.
1 Set the gantry to 0°, then attach the front pointer.
2 Use the front pointer to set the treatment table to the isocenter.
3 Adjust the three vertical offset screws to align projected distance meter image 100 cm mark
with the crosswires.
4 Loosen the clamping brackets that hold the barrel of the distance meter and push the
distance meter back so that it is flush with the bracket.
This exposes the horizontal adjustment screws of the graticule.
5 Rotate the barrel to a position where there is no horizontal change in the distance between
the projected distance meter image and the crosswire when the treatment table is raised
and lowered through its full range (170 cm to 85 cm SSD).
6 When the angular position of the barrel is established, adjust the graticule horizontally by
using the two side ways adjustment screws, such that the projected distance meter image is
within 2 mm of the edge of the crosswire image throughout the full range of the ODI.
7 Do a check of the gain calibration of the scale. Use the treatment table and a tape measure.
8 Make sure that the scale of the ODI agrees with the scale of the treatment table to within
2 mm throughout its range, and within 1 mm at the isocenter.
9 If it does not agree, increase or decrease the gain. Loosen the 10 mm clamp bolt and move
the lamp up or down as necessary.
10 Do a check again of all the mechanical adjustments until a stable set up is achieved, since
any adjustment can slightly offset another.
